retrieved_docs.title: Ramsay Hunt Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Generally, the most apparent feature on physical examination will be unilateral hemifacial palsy, which will be complete in close to 50% of patients. [14] Facial analysis may reveal the absence of transverse forehead rhytides, brow ptosis, lagophthalmos, ectropion, effacement of the nasolabial fold, the collapse of the nasal valve, malposition of the oral commissure inferiorly, and absence of platysmal banding on the affected side. The philtrum and nasal base will also likely shift away from the paralyzed side. Examination of a facial paralysis patient should be performed in three distinct passes. The first should look for asymmetry at rest, evaluating the upper third of the face, followed by the middle, and then the lower face and neck. The second should assess each of the major extratemporal branches of the facial nerve in sequence: frontal by brow elevation, zygomatic by gentle and forceful eye closure, buccal by smiling, marginal mandibular by depression of the lower lip, and cervical by platysmal contraction. Evaluating each of these movements also makes grading and documentation using the House-Brackmann scale straightforward. In the House-Brackmann system, a grade I represents normal facial function, grade II slight asymmetry with movement but complete eye closure with gentle effort, grade III more asymmetry with movement but complete eye closure with maximal effort only, grade IV still greater asymmetry with movement and inability to close the eye completely, grade V gross resting asymmetry and inability to close the eye, and grade VI demonstrates no movement at all. [29] Numerous other clinical facial nerve grading systems are available, but the House-Brackmann scale remains the most widely employed due to its ease of use. [30] [31] [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Palate -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Repeated bouts of upper airway obstruction cause obstructive sleep apnea during the deep stages of sleep as a result of the narrowing/collapse of the respiratory passages. Patients with obstructive sleep apnea tend to be overweight with associated peripharyngeal fat infiltration and/or increased size of the tongue and soft palate. These anatomic abnormalities predispose patients to obstructive sleep apnea because they decrease the cross-sectional area of the upper airway. An increased neck circumference corresponds to increased peripharyngeal fat infiltration, specifically lining the airway and at the base of the tongue. Neck circumference should be measured when obstructive sleep apnea is suspected. A neck circumference greater than seventeen inches in men and greater than sixteen inches in women increases the risk for obstructive sleep apnea. One local airway factor thought to be a causative agent of obstructive sleep apnea is an elongated soft palate. A distal palatal segment length greater than fifteen millimeters has shown to increase the risk of sleep apnea dramatically. Initial treatment of sleep apnea is primarily nonsurgical and consists of positive airway pressure therapy, most commonly in the form of CPAP. Historically, many of the surgical techniques used to correct sleep apnea in adults were sub-optimal, and palatopharyngoplasty techniques have evolved from primarily excisional methods to those that reposition and reconstruct the palate. Examples of these more advanced surgical techniques include the expansion sphincter-pharyngoplasty, lateral pharyngoplasty, relocation pharyngoplasty, and uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP). [1] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Chin Augmentation -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: A thorough assessment of the chin should encompass evaluation in 3 dimensions—vertical (superior-inferior), horizontal (anterior-posterior), and transverse dimensions. In addition, the chin's position relative to the soft tissues of the lips, teeth, maxilla, nose, and neck should be considered. While photographic analysis suffices for minor deformities necessitating an alloplastic implant, more significant deformities, such as vertical height excess or transverse asymmetry, may require radiographic evaluation. Several techniques exist to help evaluate patients who may benefit from a chin augmentation: In Goode's method, a line perpendicular to the Frankfort horizontal line passes through the alar groove. According to this approach, the pogonion (the most prominent or anterior point of chin projection) should align with this line or be situated immediately posterior to it.

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Chin Augmentation -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Although these methods provide valuable insight into achieving an ideal chin position concerning the lips, face, and neck, only a few account for the crucial relationship between the nose and chin. This relationship is vital to acknowledge, as they directly influence each other's relative appearance in space. As a general rule, a more projected nose tends to make the chin appear less projected, and vice versa. An essential and ideal harmonious balance exists between the projection of the chin and the nose. Notably, all the mentioned analysis methods rely on standardized photography techniques, with four of them utilizing the Frankfort horizontal line—a well-established anatomical reference extending from the superior border of the external auditory canal to the inferior orbital rim. [5] Furthermore, radiographic evaluation, including panoramic radiographs and/or lateral and anterior-posterior (AP) radiographs, may be beneficial for addressing more complex deformities such as vertical height excess, orthognathic abnormalities, and transverse asymmetry, commonly observed in conditions such as hemifacial/craniofacial microsomia, oculoauricular vertebral (OAV) syndrome, or isolated anatomical components.

retrieved_docs.title: Mid Forehead Brow Lift -- Preparation -- Patient Consultation

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertelorism -- Treatment / Management -- Facial bipartition (Van der Meulen Technique)
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians use the facial bipartition or the Van der Meulen  technique when hypertelorism is present along with narrowing of the maxillary arch as in Apert syndrome. [26] [27] The procedure medializes and rotates the orbits, and lateralizes and expands the maxillary arch. The surgical approach involves making vertical osteotomies or bone cuts on either side of the facial midline, separating the midface into 2 distinct segments. Once separated, the facial halves are rotated inwards towards the midline, allowing for the narrowing of the orbital distance and correction of the width of the maxillary arch. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Crouzon Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The underdevelopment of the maxilla and zygomatic arches causes midface hypoplasia, a defining characteristic of Crouzon syndrome. [4] This anomaly leads to aesthetic concerns and contributes to functional impairments, such as malocclusion and upper airway obstruction. [5] Proptosis, hypertelorism, and vision impairment result from orbital dysmorphology, which leads to exposure keratopathy and optic atrophy. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Upper Airway Resistance Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Oral appliances offer an effective alternative for patients with UARS who are intolerant of CPAP or decline surgical intervention (see Image . Custom Oral Appliance for Upper Airway Resistance Syndrome). [22] These devices advance the mandible and soft tissue anteriorly, increasing the airway space posterior to the tongue. Long-term use, such as for 1.5 years, has been associated with reductions in stress-related symptoms, as measured by the Inventory of Stress Symptoms. [23] Additional oral appliance options include tongue-retaining devices, soft palate lifters, and tongue stabilizers.

retrieved_docs.title: Pierre Robin Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Micrognathia
retrieved_docs.content: This is a clinical diagnosis of the underdeveloped mandible. This typically includes a shorter mandibular body length and a greater mandibular angle. Defined indexes for diagnosing micrognathia have been developed in the past. However, none have been useful in predicting patients who will experience respiratory problems.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertelorism -- Treatment / Management -- Monobloc advancement
retrieved_docs.content: Monobloc advancement involves repositioning and forward advancement of the entire front of the skull. This technique is useful for patients with severe craniofacial deformities. Osteotomies are made along the facial skeleton, detaching the midface from the skull. Then, clinicians move the entire facial structure to narrow the space between the eyes and correct other facial features.

Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1843
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation are classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني, which denotes fetal growth restriction. Recognizing that تقييد النمو الجنيني falls within this larger perinatal context helps clinicians assess and manage infants presenting with these growth‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions that influence labor and delivery encompass a broad spectrum of obstetric complications, uterine functional abnormalities, cervical pathology, multiple‑gestation pregnancies, fetal size disproportion, abnormal fetal presentations, and drug contraindications in pregnancy. Abnormal fetal presentations—breech malpresentation, face or brow presentation, mentum (chin‑first) presentation, transverse or oblique lie, arm presentation (سوء المجيء الجنيني, تدلّي ذراع الجنين), shoulder presentation (وضعية المَجيء الكتفي), funic presentation, and other malpositions or unstable fetal positions—can impede vaginal descent and often require operative delivery or specialized obstetric management; they are linked to prolonged first‑stage or second‑stage labor, failure to induce labor, uterine hypotonia (رحم ناقص التوتر), secondary uterine inertia (عطالة رحمية ثانوية), and prolapse of the fetal arm. Anterior and posterior asynclitism represent specific uterine functional

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2314
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition; malpresentation is classified as a protracted (prolapsed) arm of the fetus, with the episode of care unspecified or not applicable.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is a specific fetal presentation that has as its parent the broader category Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable. This broader category is classified as a Malposition; malpresentation, indicating that the fetus is positioned abnormally in the birth canal. Understanding that both Face OR brow presentation of fetus and the unspecified Face or brow presentation fall under the umbrella of malposition or malpresentation informs clinicians that these presentations may complicate labor and delivery, prompting careful obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1979
retrieved_docs.content: Early or threatened labor is classified under the Arabic term “تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر”. Clinical observations indicate that short stature (قصر القامة) is associated with this condition, suggesting that patients with reduced height may have an increased risk of experiencing early or threatened labor. This relationship underscores the importance of evaluating stature when assessing obstetric risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1075
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is a specific fetal presentation that is linked hierarchically to the more general term “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” indicating that the former is a child concept of the latter. This generic face‑or‑brow presentation is classified as a type of malposition; malpresentation, placing it within the broader category of abnormal fetal positions. Similarly, Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, and Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus are each connected to their respective generic descriptors (“Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable”) through a “has parent” relationship, showing that the specific fetal findings are sub‑categories of the unnamed generic forms. retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized as a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that influence the management of the mother, and it specifically encompasses the sub‑condition of cervical shortening occurring in the second trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2901
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation is recognized as a specific perinatal condition that is classified as تقييد النمو الجنيني, indicating fetal growth restriction, and also as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}, denoting a birth at the highest degree of prematurity with an unspecified weight. This dual classification underscores the clinical importance of identifying both the limited fetal growth and the extreme prematurity associated with these presentations for appropriate medical management and follow‑up.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often experience growth restriction that results in reduced adult height. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this context reflects severe underlying health challenges that increase the risk of death shortly after birth. Recognizing the link between low‑birth‑weight preterm infants, subsequent short stature, and heightened neonatal mortality can guide clinicians in early monitoring and intervention strategies to improve outcomes for this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior asynclitism and posterior asynclitism are distinct subtypes of uterine function, each describing a specific asynclitic presentation of the fetal head during labor. Both conditions are considered child concepts within the broader category of uterine function, reflecting variations in fetal head alignment that can influence the mechanics of childbirth and guide obstetric assessment and management.
